Lich.signature: A CR 21 spellcaster with legendary actions, Legendary Resistance, and a phylactery that means killing it is only temporary — the real encounter is finding and destroying the phylactery.
abilities: Spellcasting (18th-level): Power Word Kill (instant death at <100 HP), Disintegrate, Globe of Invulnerability, Counterspell, Fireball. Full wizard list with 9th-level slots.
Legendary Actions (3/round): Cantrip (free damage), Paralyzing Touch (CON save or paralyzed), Frightening Gaze (WIS save or frightened AND paralyzed). Acts between every party turn.
Legendary Resistance (3/Day): Auto-succeeds three saves. The party's best spells simply fail three times.
Rejuvenation: If it has a phylactery, a destroyed lich reforms in 1d10 days. Killing it without destroying the phylactery accomplishes nothing.
Lich.tactical_identity: Paranoid archmage who has had centuries to prepare. Layers defenses, uses Globe of Invulnerability to ignore low-level spells, and Power Word Kills anyone who drops below 100 HP. The lich always has an escape plan.
encounter_hooks: the phylactery is the real objective — the lich itself is just the guardian, and it reforms if the phylactery survives
centuries of preparation means lair defenses, contingency spells, and minions positioned throughout
Power Word Kill has no save — any character below 100 HP is at risk of instant death
Lich.description: A skeletal figure in rotting robes, pinpoints of cold light burning in its empty eye sockets. An ancient wizard who chose undeath over mortality — patient, paranoid, and always planning centuries ahead.
Lich.retreat: Teleports or uses contingency spells to escape. Reforms at phylactery in 1d10 days. Always has an exit plan.
Lich.social: Speaks with cold intellectual superiority. May negotiate if the party possesses something it needs. Considers mortals beneath it but useful.
aftermath_hooks: the phylactery was not here — the lich reforms in 1d10 days and the real objective is finding and destroying it
the lich's research notes reveal what it was trying to achieve — the project continues without it
a servant or ally of the lich is already en route to check on its master

Vampire.signature: Charm, Bite that reduces max HP and creates vampire spawn, legendary actions, Regeneration, and a suite of weaknesses — a vampire encounter is a gothic puzzle of strengths and vulnerabilities.
abilities: Charm: WIS save or charmed. Charmed targets are willing allies. Can turn party members against each other.
Bite: On a grappled/restrained target, massive damage + max HP reduction + heals the vampire. Bite kills create vampire spawn under its control.
Legendary Actions (3/round): Move (no opportunity attacks), Unarmed Strike, Bite (costs 2). Constant pressure between party turns.
Vampire Weaknesses: Forbiddance (can't enter uninvited), Harmed by Running Water, Stake to the Heart (paralyzes in resting place), Sunlight Hypersensitivity (20 radiant/round in sunlight).
Vampire.tactical_identity: Charismatic predator that charms one party member, uses Legendary Actions to reposition and bite, and regenerates constantly. Fights in its lair at night where weaknesses don't apply. Retreats to its coffin if threatened.
encounter_hooks: Charm can turn a party member into a temporary ally — the party fights itself before fighting the vampire
vampire weaknesses create non-combat victory conditions — sunlight, running water, finding the coffin
Bite kills create vampire spawn — NPCs killed by the vampire may appear as enemies in later encounters
Vampire.description: Pale, aristocratic, and unnervingly beautiful, with an intensity that makes eye contact feel like a trap. A predator who prefers seduction and manipulation to violence — every conversation is a hunt.
Vampire.retreat: Mists to 0 HP and reforms at coffin in 1 hour. Must find and destroy the coffin to truly kill it.
Vampire.social: Charming and aristocratic. Invites the party as guests. Every kindness is manipulation toward isolation and feeding.
aftermath_hooks: the coffin must be found before dawn or the vampire regenerates at full HP
a charmed NPC the party knows is still under the vampire's influence
a letter from the vampire's living descendants who don't know what their ancestor became

Doppelganger.signature: Shapechanger + Read Thoughts means the doppelganger knows what the party expects to see and becomes it — the encounter starts long before the party realizes there's a fight.
abilities: Shapechanger: Polymorphs into any Small or Medium humanoid. The disguise is physical, not magical — True Seeing doesn't reveal it.
Read Thoughts: Reads surface thoughts within 60 ft. Knows what the party suspects, fears, and plans.
Ambusher: Advantage on attack rolls against surprised creatures. The doppelganger's first strike is devastating.
Multiattack: Two slam attacks. Decent melee but the real weapon is deception.
Doppelganger.tactical_identity: Infiltrator that replaces an NPC or party ally, reads thoughts to maintain the disguise, and strikes when trust is highest. Combat is its failure state — it wins by never being discovered.
encounter_hooks: Read Thoughts means it knows if the party suspects it — it adjusts behavior accordingly
the real encounter is paranoia — once the party knows doppelgangers exist, they suspect everyone
physical shapechange (not magical) means Detect Magic doesn't work — only careful observation catches it
Doppelganger.description: In its true form, a gaunt gray humanoid with blank features. It is almost never in its true form — it becomes whoever you expect to see, and it has read your thoughts to know exactly who that is.
Doppelganger.retreat: Reverts to a new disguise and walks away in the confusion. Or surrenders and offers secrets.
Doppelganger.social: In disguise, mirrors what the party expects. If exposed, bargains desperately — names who hired it and who else it has replaced.
aftermath_hooks: the real person it was impersonating — dead, imprisoned, or missing somewhere nearby
notes or payment revealing who hired it and what information it was gathering
the party can't confirm it was the only one — were there others in town?

Oni.signature: Change Shape + Invisibility + Regeneration — an oni disguises itself as a traveler, turns invisible to reposition, and regenerates 10 HP per round. It's a trickster brute.
abilities: Change Shape: Polymorphs into Small or Medium humanoid. The classic 'wounded traveler' ambush.
Innate Spellcasting: Invisibility (at will), Darkness, Charm Person, Cone of Cold (1/day), Gaseous Form.
Regeneration: 10 HP per round. Only stops if it takes fire or acid damage.
Glaive: Reach 10 ft, 2d10+4 damage. Huge melee range with devastating damage.
Oni.tactical_identity: Cunning ambusher that disguises itself to lure prey close, drops the disguise for a devastating first strike, and uses Invisibility to reposition when threatened. Regeneration means it outlasts most parties in a straight fight.
encounter_hooks: Change Shape as a wounded traveler is the classic oni ambush — passive Perception 15+ spots the lack of actual wounds
Invisibility at will means it vanishes and repositions whenever the party gains advantage
Regeneration + Invisibility means it can disengage, heal, and return — the fight can span multiple rounds of cat-and-mouse
Oni.description: In true form, a towering blue-skinned ogre with horns and tusks. Usually disguised as a wounded traveler or lost merchant — the friendly stranger on the road who is too good to be true.
Oni.retreat: Turns invisible or flies away. Returns in a new disguise to infiltrate the party's allies.
Oni.social: Disguised as a friendly giant, merchant, or official. Charming and generous until it feeds. Prefers to isolate victims from the group.
aftermath_hooks: the oni had replaced someone in a position of trust — that person's body is hidden nearby
local disappearances match the oni's feeding pattern — it has been here for weeks or months
the oni's true lair contains trophies from victims and a larder of preserved humanoid flesh

Medusa.signature: Petrifying Gaze turns creatures to stone permanently — like the basilisk but the medusa is intelligent, uses a bow, and fights tactically while forcing the party to avert their eyes.
abilities: Petrifying Gaze: Creatures starting their turn within 30 ft that can see the medusa's eyes must CON DC 14 save or begin turning to stone. Two failures = permanently petrified.
Snake Hair: Melee attack with poison damage. Punishes anyone who gets close.
Longbow: The medusa doesn't need melee. It shoots while the party fights with eyes closed.
Medusa.tactical_identity: Ranged combatant who forces a terrible choice: look at me (risk petrification) or avert your eyes (disadvantage on attacks). Shoots with a longbow while the party stumbles blind, closing to melee only against petrified targets.
encounter_hooks: the look-or-don't-look dilemma has no good answer — the party must find creative solutions like mirrors or blindfolds
petrified statues of previous victims decorate the lair — some might be NPCs the party knows
intelligent (INT 12) and vain — can be negotiated with if the party offers something it values
Medusa.description: A woman whose beauty survives despite the writhing nest of snakes where her hair should be. Vain and intelligent — she collects petrified admirers like statues in a gallery.
Medusa.retreat: Flees behind petrified victims for cover. May negotiate from a position of threat.
Medusa.social: Bitter about her curse. May seek help breaking it. Alternatively, has embraced it and collects petrified trophies with pride.
aftermath_hooks: the petrified figures can be restored — one of them has information or is someone important
a journal revealing the medusa was cursed by a specific entity — breaking the curse is possible but requires a quest
one petrified figure is posed protectively over something — whatever it was shielding is still there

Night Hag.signature: Nightmare Haunting targets a sleeping creature, preventing rest and reducing max HP — the night hag wins by never fighting, visiting the party every night until they die of exhaustion.
abilities: Nightmare Haunting: While ethereal, touches a sleeping creature. Target has nightmares, gains no benefit from rest, and max HP decreases. At 0 max HP, the target dies and the hag captures the soul.
Etherealness: Shifts to the Ethereal Plane at will. The party can't touch her unless they can reach the Ethereal Plane.
Change Shape: Any Medium female humanoid. Can infiltrate and observe.
Heartstone: Makes the hag ethereal. Stealing it removes her escape route.
Night Hag.tactical_identity: Patient predator that never needs to fight. Haunts a target nightly from the Ethereal Plane, slowly killing them through exhaustion and lost max HP. Only vulnerable if the party finds a way to reach the Ethereal Plane or steal the Heartstone.
encounter_hooks: the party can't rest safely until the hag is dealt with — long rest denial is devastating over multiple days
ethereal means she's untouchable by normal means — the party needs specific spells or items
hags often trade souls and deals — negotiation may be more effective than combat
Night Hag.description: A withered crone who rarely appears in her true form, preferring Disguise Self to infiltrate and observe. A dealmaker who trades in souls and nightmares — she would rather corrupt the party than fight them.
Night Hag.retreat: Flees via Etherealness when threatened. Returns on the Ethereal Plane to haunt the party nightly.
Night Hag.social: Offers a bargain — dark knowledge, a favor, or a curse removed — in exchange for something the party will regret giving.
aftermath_hooks: the Heartstone — stealing it prevents Ethereal escape but marks the thief for nightmare haunting
a soul bag containing trapped souls that whisper names the party recognizes
a ledger of bargains with locals — someone in the nearest town still owes the hag a debt

Green Hag.signature: Illusory Appearance + Mimicry + Invisible Passage — a green hag deceives, imitates voices, and turns invisible. The encounter is a series of tricks, not a straight fight.
abilities: Illusory Appearance: Looks like any Medium female humanoid. Physically touching her reveals the illusion (DC 20 Investigation to see through otherwise).
Invisible Passage: Turns invisible until it attacks or uses a spell. At-will invisibility for repositioning.
Mimicry: Imitates animal sounds and humanoid voices perfectly. Lures victims with familiar calls.
Claws: Melee attack. The hag is dangerous in melee but prefers not to fight directly.
Green Hag.tactical_identity: Deceiver that lures victims with Mimicry, disguises herself with Illusory Appearance, and uses Invisible Passage to escape or reposition. Prefers to trick the party into dangerous situations rather than fight directly.
encounter_hooks: Mimicry can imitate a party member's voice calling for help — the party splits up to investigate
Illusory Appearance means the 'helpful old woman' in the swamp might be the encounter
green hags love bargains — they'll offer something the party needs in exchange for something terrible
Green Hag.description: Appears as a decrepit woman with green-tinged skin and wild hair, but uses illusions to look like anyone. Prefers tricks and manipulation over confrontation — her voice can mimic anyone.
Green Hag.retreat: Turns invisible and flees. Returns in a new disguise to torment the party socially.
Green Hag.social: Disguised as a harmless old woman or helpful herbalist. Offers aid with hidden costs. Delights in corrupting bargains.
aftermath_hooks: the hag's lair contains jars of preserved memories, dreams, or voices — one belongs to someone the party knows
a mirror that shows its viewer as old, sick, or dying — it's cursed but the curse is subtle
the hag had been impersonating someone in a nearby village — that person is missing or dead

Sea Hag.signature: Horrific Appearance forces a CON save or the creature drops to 0 HP — a sea hag can instant-kill a low-level character just by revealing its true face.
abilities: Horrific Appearance: Creatures within 30 ft that can see the hag's true form must CON DC 11 save or be frightened. If they fail by 5+, they drop to 0 HP instead.
Death Glare: One frightened creature within 30 ft must WIS DC 11 save or drop to 0 HP. Combo with Horrific Appearance.
Claws: Basic melee. Not the real threat.
Sea Hag.tactical_identity: Ambush predator that reveals its true face to frighten the party, then uses Death Glare to drop frightened targets to 0 HP. Low CR but the instant-down mechanic makes it lethal against unprepared parties.
encounter_hooks: Horrific Appearance + Death Glare can drop a character from full HP to 0 in one round — terrifying at low levels
sea hags are encountered near water where drowning an unconscious character is trivially easy
hag covens (three hags together) gain shared spellcasting — a sea hag in a coven is far more dangerous
Sea Hag.description: Gaunt and waterlogged, with matted seaweed hair and eyes like dead fish. Her horrific appearance is itself a weapon — meeting her gaze can frighten a creature to death.
Sea Hag.retreat: Submerges and flees through water. May call aquatic minions to cover retreat.
Sea Hag.social: Bitter and envious. Demands tribute of beauty — a lock of hair, a song, a treasured memory. Curses those who refuse.
aftermath_hooks: a collection of stolen beautiful things — portraits, jewelry, instruments — each taken from someone the hag envied
fishermen have been paying tribute to avoid the hag's gaze — the tribute demands escalated recently
the hag's death lifts a curse on local waters — fish return, storms calm, but something the hag was keeping away may also return

Flameskull.signature: Rejuvenation means a destroyed flameskull reforms in 1 hour unless holy water is sprinkled on its remains — the party 'wins' the fight but the skull comes back behind them.
abilities: Rejuvenation: Reforms in 1 hour with full HP unless destroyed with holy water or Dispel Magic/Remove Curse on the remains.
Fireball: 8d6 fire in a 20-ft radius. A flying skull casting Fireball is terrifying.
Shield: Reaction for +5 AC. Hard to hit when it matters.
Illumination: Sheds light. Ironically, the skull itself lights up the room.
Flameskull.tactical_identity: Flying artillery that hovers out of melee reach and casts Fireball. If destroyed, reforms in an hour — the party must know the holy water trick or fight it again. Immune to exhaustion and doesn't sleep — it's the perfect guardian.
encounter_hooks: Rejuvenation means killing it isn't enough — the party must either know the trick or face it again later
fly speed keeps it out of melee — ranged attacks and spells are the only reliable options
a flameskull guarding a location has been doing so for potentially centuries — it knows every corridor
Flameskull.description: A floating humanoid skull wreathed in green or orange flames. The remnant of a spellcaster bound to a duty it continues beyond death — it often still believes it is alive.
aftermath_hooks: Rejuvenation — the flameskull reforms in 1 hour unless destroyed by holy water or dispel magic on the remains
the flameskull was programmed with specific orders by its creator — the orders reveal the creator's priorities
the flameskull's fire spells damaged something in the area — a book, a map, a door mechanism

Nothic.signature: Weird Insight reads a creature's secrets (contested Deception vs. Insight) — a nothic knows the party's hidden truths and trades that information for food or trinkets.
abilities: Weird Insight: Targets one creature. Contested check. Success = the nothic learns one fact or secret about the target. No save — just a skill contest.
Rotting Gaze: 10 (3d6) necrotic damage, CON DC 12 save for half. Ranged necrotic beam.
Multiattack: Two claw attacks in melee. Decent damage up close.
Nothic.tactical_identity: Paranoid information broker that hides in darkness, reads secrets with Weird Insight, and trades what it knows for food or shiny objects. Fights only when cornered — it prefers to observe and bargain.
encounter_hooks: Weird Insight makes it a blackmail encounter — it knows party secrets and may share them with enemies
nothics are former wizards driven mad — they might remember fragments of useful knowledge
can be bribed with food, magic items, or knowledge — combat is optional if the party negotiates
Nothic.description: A hunched, one-eyed creature that was once a wizard, twisted by forbidden knowledge into something pathetic and dangerous. It whispers secrets it shouldn't know and craves the magic it lost.
Nothic.retreat: Flees into darkness. Stalks the party from hiding, whispering secrets.
Nothic.social: Knows secrets through Weird Insight. Bargains with forbidden knowledge. Desperate and paranoid. May reveal truths the party doesn't want to hear.
aftermath_hooks: the nothic knew a secret about one of the party members — it whispered it during the fight, and now the party has to deal with it
the nothic was once a wizard — its lair contains remnants of arcane research that drove it mad
the nothic's Weird Insight revealed something about an NPC or location the party hasn't visited yet

Banshee.signature: Wail drops every creature within 30 feet to 0 HP on a failed CON save (1/day) — a banshee can TPK an entire party with a single ability.
abilities: Wail (1/Day): Every creature within 30 ft makes CON DC 13 or drops to 0 HP. Creatures that succeed take 3d6 psychic instead. This is effectively a TPK button.
Horrifying Visage: WIS DC 13 or frightened for 1 minute. Fail by 5+ = aged 1d4×10 years.
Corrupting Touch: 3d6+2 necrotic melee. Decent damage but the Wail is what matters.
Incorporeal Movement: Phases through walls and objects.
Banshee.tactical_identity: Glass cannon that exists to use Wail. Opens with Horrifying Visage to scatter the party, then positions for maximum Wail coverage. After Wail, it's a fragile incorporeal attacker that phases through walls to escape retaliation.
encounter_hooks: Wail is a potential TPK — the entire party must save or drop to 0 HP simultaneously
Wail works once per day — if the party knows this, they can bait it out and then fight safely
banshees are bound to a location and a regret — understanding their story may provide a non-combat solution
Banshee.description: A translucent elven woman in tattered finery, her beauty marred by grief and rage frozen on her face. The wail that announces her presence is the last sound many creatures hear.
Banshee.retreat: Becomes incorporeal and fades away. Returns at nightfall.
Banshee.social: Consumed by grief and vanity. Can be appeased with flattery about its former beauty. May answer one question truthfully if offered something it valued in life.
aftermath_hooks: the banshee's Wail can kill outright — any character dropped to 0 HP by it needs to know how close they came
the source of the banshee's grief is specific — a betrayal, a lost love, a stolen treasure — resolving it could lay the spirit to rest
the banshee's former home or possessions are nearby — they're haunted but contain something valuable

Ghost.signature: Possession takes over a party member's body completely — the ghost fights using the party member's class features, spells, and equipment against the rest of the group.
abilities: Possession: CHA DC 13 or the ghost controls the target's body completely. The ghost uses the target's stats, class features, and equipment. Turning and Protection from Evil end it.
Horrifying Visage: WIS DC 13 or frightened. Fail by 5+ = aged 1d4×10 years. Magical aging.
Etherealness: Shifts to Ethereal Plane at will. Untouchable until it chooses to interact.
Withering Touch: 4d6+3 necrotic damage. Solid melee when it's not possessing someone.
Ghost.tactical_identity: Ethereal ambusher that phases through walls, possesses the strongest party member, and uses their body to fight the rest of the group. The party must defeat their own ally without killing them.
encounter_hooks: Possession of the party fighter turns their strongest asset into their deadliest threat
Etherealness means it's untouchable between attacks — the party can only fight it when it chooses to appear
ghosts are bound by unfinished business — resolving their story destroys them without combat
Ghost.description: A translucent figure that flickers between its living appearance and the moment of its death. Bound to the world by unfinished business — it may not even realize it is dead.
Ghost.retreat: Enters Ethereal Plane and returns the next night. Cannot be permanently destroyed until its unfinished business is resolved.
Ghost.social: Fixated on its unfinished business. May communicate through visions, cold spots, or possession. Can be reasoned with if the party addresses its obsession.
aftermath_hooks: the ghost returns tomorrow night unless its unfinished business is resolved — the party now has a mystery to solve
Possession revealed something the ghost knew in life — a secret, a location, a name
the ghost's grave, remains, or personal effects are somewhere else — finding them is the key to putting it to rest

Mummy.signature: Dreadful Glare frightens AND paralyzes on a bad enough roll, and Rotting Fist inflicts a curse that prevents HP recovery — a mummy debilitates the party even if it loses the fight.
abilities: Dreadful Glare: WIS DC 11 or frightened. Fail by 5+ = paralyzed instead. Targets one creature, no action cost beyond looking at them.
Rotting Fist: On hit, CON DC 12 or cursed with mummy rot. Cursed creature can't regain HP and max HP decreases every 24 hours. Remove Curse required.
Multiattack: Two Rotting Fist attacks per turn.
Mummy.tactical_identity: Cursing bruiser that frightens targets with Dreadful Glare, then closes to melee with Rotting Fist. Even if the party wins, the mummy rot curse continues to threaten — the encounter has lasting consequences.
encounter_hooks: mummy rot prevents ALL healing and reduces max HP daily — without Remove Curse, it's a death sentence
a mummy's tomb is trapped and consecrated — the environment is as dangerous as the mummy itself
mummies serve a purpose (guarding a tomb, protecting an artifact) — understanding why it fights may help
Mummy.description: A humanoid corpse wrapped in decaying linen bandages, moving with slow deliberation and ancient purpose. It guards something — a tomb, a relic, a secret — with mindless devotion.
aftermath_hooks: the mummy's wrappings bear inscriptions identifying who it was and what it was buried to guard
disturbing the tomb triggered a curse — Mummy Rot on a wounded character won't manifest for days
the sarcophagus contains a second, smaller chamber beneath the body — something was hidden under the mummy

Mummy Lord.signature: A CR 15 spellcaster with legendary actions, Rejuvenation, and mummy rot — a mummy lord is a lich wrapped in bandages, reforming at its sarcophagus unless the heart is destroyed.
abilities: Spellcasting (10th-level cleric): Contagion, Insect Plague, Guardian of Faith, Harm, Spiritual Weapon. Devastating divine magic.
Rejuvenation: Reforms in 24 hours if its heart (hidden in a canopic jar) is intact. Like a lich's phylactery.
Legendary Actions (3/round): Blinding Dust (blind in 5 ft), Blasphemous Word (stun, costs 2), Channel Negative Energy (6d6 necrotic in 60 ft, costs 3).
Dreadful Glare + Rotting Fist: Same as regular mummy but with higher DCs.
Mummy Lord.tactical_identity: Divine lich that combines devastating cleric spells with mummy abilities and legendary actions. Insect Plague for area denial, Spiritual Weapon for bonus action damage, and Channel Negative Energy through legendary actions for massive necrotic bursts.
encounter_hooks: the canopic jar heart is the real objective — the mummy lord reforms unless it's found and destroyed
divine spellcasting means it has healing, which mummies normally don't — it sustains itself mid-fight
the tomb is the lair — traps, cursed artifacts, and undead minions fill the space between the party and the sarcophagus
Mummy Lord.description: A preserved corpse wearing the ceremonial garb of a long-dead ruler, radiating palpable dread. An intelligent undead monarch still carrying out the duties it held in life, commanding undead as it once commanded armies.
Mummy Lord.retreat: Rejuvenation reforms it in 24 hours near its heart canopic jar. Destroying the jar is the real objective.
Mummy Lord.social: Speaks as an ancient ruler. Demands obedience and worship. May offer knowledge of the ancient past in exchange for service.
aftermath_hooks: Rejuvenation — the mummy lord returns in 24 hours unless its heart is destroyed, and the heart wasn't in this room
the lord's tomb contains histories and records lost to the modern world — scholars would pay fortunes for them
the mummy lord's curse spreads beyond its tomb — crops failing, livestock dying, illness spreading in nearby settlements

Rakshasa.signature: Immune to spells of 6th level and below — a rakshasa shrugs off most of the party's spellbook. Only 7th+ level spells and the vulnerability to blessed crossbow bolts can reliably hurt it.
abilities: Limited Magic Immunity: Can't be affected or detected by spells of 6th level or lower unless it chooses. Fireball, Hold Person, Counterspell — all useless.
Innate Spellcasting: Detect Thoughts, Disguise Self, Dominate Person, Plane Shift. Reads minds, infiltrates, controls.
Vulnerability: Piercing from magic weapons wielded by good-aligned creatures. Blessed crossbow bolts are the classic answer.
Shapechanger: Any humanoid form. Perfect infiltrator.
Rakshasa.tactical_identity: Master infiltrator and manipulator immune to most magic. Uses Disguise Self and Detect Thoughts to impersonate trusted NPCs, then Dominate Person to control party members. Only fights when its cover is blown, and Plane Shifts away if losing.
encounter_hooks: magic immunity below 7th level makes it nearly untouchable by most parties — martial characters with magic weapons are essential
Disguise Self + Detect Thoughts means it could be impersonating anyone the party trusts
Plane Shift escape means killing a rakshasa requires trapping it first — it won't stay to die
Rakshasa.description: Appears as a well-dressed noble with the head of a tiger and hands whose palms face the wrong way. A fiend disguised as high society — charming, cultured, and utterly evil.
Rakshasa.retreat: Plane shifts to the Nine Hells. Returns in a new disguise to torment the party. Only truly dies if killed on the Nine Hells.
Rakshasa.social: Disguised as a noble, merchant, or advisor. Perfectly charming. Manipulates through social power, wealth, and political connections.
aftermath_hooks: the rakshasa is banished, not dead — it reforms on the Nine Hells and will return in weeks with a vendetta
its network of agents, servants, and blackmailed officials remains intact — the power structure doesn't collapse with one death
documents in its lair reveal it has been manipulating local politics for years — the consequences of its schemes are already in motion

Succubus/Incubus.signature: Charm + Draining Kiss — the succubus charms a target into willingness, then kisses them to drain max HP. The encounter is seduction and betrayal, not combat.
abilities: Charm: WIS DC 15 or charmed for 1 day. The target obeys the succubus's verbal or telepathic commands. High DC makes this stick.
Draining Kiss: A willing or charmed creature is kissed. 5d10+5 psychic damage and max HP reduced by the same amount. Can kill through HP drain.
Shapechanger: Any Medium humanoid form. Approaches as an attractive, trustworthy stranger.
Etherealness: Escapes to the Ethereal Plane if threatened.
Succubus/Incubus.tactical_identity: Seducer that avoids combat entirely. Charms a target, isolates them from the party, and drains them with Draining Kiss. If discovered, uses Etherealness to flee. Never fights when it can manipulate.
encounter_hooks: the encounter is social, not combat — the party must identify the succubus before it isolates and drains a target
Charm at DC 15 sticks on most characters — the party doesn't know their ally has been compromised
Etherealness means it escapes if discovered — the party must trap it or solve the problem socially
Succubus/Incubus.description: Takes the form of whatever its target finds most attractive. A fiend that feeds on desire and corrupts through intimacy — by the time its true nature is revealed, the damage is done.
Succubus/Incubus.retreat: Plane shifts to escape. Returns in a new form to seduce and corrupt different targets.
Succubus/Incubus.social: Appears as whatever the target most desires. Offers companionship, love, or alliance. The goal is always corruption of the soul.
aftermath_hooks: the succubus's previous victims are still alive but corrupted — one is in a position of power nearby
a charmed NPC doesn't realize they've been manipulated — breaking the news is its own challenge
the fiend was sent by a more powerful patron — it was gathering something specific: secrets, souls, or influence

Hydra.signature: Reactive Heads give it one reaction per head for opportunity attacks, and severed heads grow back as two — a hydra punishes melee engagement and gets stronger as the fight continues.
abilities: Multiple Heads: One attack per head (starts with 5). Opportunity attack per head. Advantage on saves against being blinded, charmed, deafened, frightened, stunned, knocked unconscious.
Reactive Heads: One reaction per head for opportunity attacks. Running past a hydra provokes FIVE attacks of opportunity.
Head Regrowth: A severed head grows back as two at start of hydra's turn unless the stump takes fire damage. The hydra escalates.
Wakeful: While sleeping, at least one head is always awake. Can't be surprised.
Hydra.tactical_identity: Escalating brute that punishes melee approach with multiple opportunity attacks and grows more dangerous as the fight continues. Fire is essential to prevent head regrowth — without it, the hydra becomes exponentially more lethal.
encounter_hooks: fire damage prevents head regrowth — a party without fire damage faces a hydra that keeps getting more heads and attacks
5 opportunity attacks means running past or away from a hydra is suicidal — the party must commit to the fight
head regrowth makes the hydra stronger when damaged — a unique dynamic where hurting it makes things worse
Hydra.description: A massive reptilian body with multiple serpentine heads, each acting with its own hungry will. Cut one head off and two grow back — the fight gets worse the longer it goes.
aftermath_hooks: if any head was cut without cauterizing, two grew back — the hydra may have grown during the fight, and its body reflects that
the hydra's lair is at a water source — the water is now safe but may have magical properties from the hydra's blood
local legends about the hydra contain a grain of truth about something else living in the same lake or swamp

Bulette.signature: Burrow 40 ft lets it attack from underground — a bulette erupts from below, attacks, and burrows again. The party can't predict where the next attack comes from.
abilities: Deadly Leap: If it jumps 15+ ft, lands adjacent to a creature — STR/DEX DC 16 or prone + 3d6+4+3d6 damage. Devastating opening attack.
Bite: 4d12+4 damage. One of the highest single-bite damages in the game.
Standing Leap: Jumps 30 ft long, 15 ft high. Can leap over the party's front line.
Burrow 40 ft: Moves through earth freely. Attacks from below, retreats below.
Bulette.tactical_identity: Ambush predator that burrows underground, erupts with Deadly Leap for massive damage, bites a prone target, then burrows again. The party fights something they can't see between attacks.
encounter_hooks: burrowing means the party can't predict where it surfaces — Tremorsense 60 ft means it always knows where they are
Deadly Leap is devastating — 3d6+4+3d6 just from landing on someone, then a 4d12+4 bite on the prone target
in open ground the bulette has total advantage — fighting near rock or structures limits its burrowing
Bulette.description: A heavily armored beast the size of a wagon that bursts from the ground like a landshark. It leaps, it digs, it bites — there is no subtlety, only appetite.
Bulette.retreat: Burrows deep underground to escape. Extremely difficult to pursue.
aftermath_hooks: the bulette's tunnel destabilized the ground — a road, building, or bridge above may collapse
the bulette's burrow intersects with a cave system or underground structure previously unknown
the bulette was attracted to a specific area by vibrations — the source of those vibrations is still active

Roper.signature: Tendrils grapple at 50-foot range and reel creatures in — a roper is a cave-ceiling ambush predator that grabs the party from extreme distance and pulls them into its bite.
abilities: False Appearance: Looks like a stalactite or stalagmite while motionless. DC 20 Perception to spot.
Tendril: 50-foot reach, grapples on hit, and the target is restrained. The roper has 6 tendrils.
Reel: Each turn, pulls each grappled creature 25 ft closer. Being dragged toward a mouth on the ceiling.
Bite: 4d8+4 damage against creatures within 5 ft. The payoff for reeling victims in.
Roper.tactical_identity: Ceiling-mounted ambush predator that disguises itself as a stalactite, grabs the party from 50 feet with multiple tendrils, and reels them toward its mouth. The fight is about escaping the grapple, not trading blows.
encounter_hooks: 50-foot tendril reach means the party is grabbed long before they realize the 'stalactite' is alive
6 tendrils means it can grab most of the party simultaneously — everyone is being reeled in at once
cutting tendrils (AC 20, 10 HP each) frees individual characters but the tendril regrows — prioritize the body
Roper.description: Looks exactly like a stalagmite until its single eye opens and six tendrils whip out. The perfect ambush predator — patient enough to wait days for prey to wander close.
aftermath_hooks: the roper's stomach contains undigested metal objects — coins, weapons, a shield with a recognizable crest
the cavern ceiling has multiple roper-shaped formations — the party can't tell which are real without testing
the roper was positioned at a chokepoint — whatever is beyond it was protected, not trapped

Cloaker.signature: Attach wraps around a creature, making the cloaker indistinguishable from the target — attacks against the cloaker hit the engulfed creature instead. The party can't fight it without hurting their ally.
abilities: Damage Transfer: While attached to a creature, the cloaker takes only half the damage dealt to it. The other half goes to the wrapped target.
False Appearance: Looks like a dark leather cloak while motionless. The party might literally put it on.
Moan: Each non-aberration within 60 ft must WIS DC 13 or become frightened. Area fear effect.
Phantasms: Creates 1d4+1 illusory duplicates if in dim light or darkness. The party doesn't know which is real.
Cloaker.tactical_identity: Ceiling ambusher that drops onto a target and wraps around them like a cloak. Damage transfer makes it a hostage situation — hitting the cloaker hurts the wrapped party member. Phantasms in darkness create confusion about which cloaker is real.
encounter_hooks: Damage Transfer creates a 'hostage' situation — the party must find a way to remove it without killing the wrapped ally
False Appearance as a cloak means the party might pick it up — mimics for your body, not your treasure
Phantasms in darkness mean the party fights 5+ cloakers when only one is real — Light spell is essential
Cloaker.description: A dark, ray-like creature that hangs from cave ceilings disguised as a cloak or leather hanging. It drops onto victims, engulfing them completely — the party sees a friend suddenly struggling inside a living blanket.
Cloaker.retreat: Creates illusory duplicates and flees while the party fights phantoms.
aftermath_hooks: the cloaker's moan caused fear or disorientation that lingers — a party member may be shaken for hours
the cavern contains multiple cloak-shaped objects — cloaks, tapestries, rugs — the party can't trust any of them
the cloaker was guarding an egg clutch — the young will hatch in days

Gibbering Mouther.signature: Gibbering forces WIS saves or creatures can't take reactions and must move randomly — the battlefield descends into chaos as the party loses control of their own movement.
abilities: Gibbering: All creatures within 20 ft at start of their turn make WIS DC 10 or can't take reactions and move randomly. The party stumbles around unable to coordinate.
Aberrant Ground: Ground within 10 ft is difficult terrain AND liquefies. Creatures starting their turn in it must STR DC 10 or sink until restrained.
Blinding Spittle: Ranged attack, target is blinded until end of mouther's next turn.
Multiattack: One bite attack that can auto-grapple.
Gibbering Mouther.tactical_identity: Area denial horror that turns the ground to liquid and scrambles nearby creatures' minds. Doesn't need tactics — its passive auras create chaos. The party fights their own movement and sanity more than the mouther itself.
encounter_hooks: Gibbering + Aberrant Ground means the party can't move deliberately or react properly — fighting it is fighting chaos
the liquefied ground means getting close risks being swallowed by the floor itself
in a narrow corridor, Gibbering affects the entire party — open spaces give room to keep distance
Gibbering Mouther.description: A heaving mound of eyes, mouths, and half-absorbed flesh that babbles constantly in dozens of voices. An aberration that dissolves the ground beneath it and drives nearby creatures insane with its gibbering.
aftermath_hooks: the ground the mouther occupied is permanently aberrant — the earth remains soft and eye-covered for days
the mouther's gibbering revealed fragments of things it absorbed — names, places, phrases from consumed victims
the mouther formed from concentrated madness or corruption — the source of that corruption is still active nearby

Otyugh.signature: Tentacle grapple + STR 16 + disease from Bite — an otyugh grabs creatures with long tentacles, pulls them into its filthy maw, and infects them with a wasting disease.
abilities: Multiattack: Two tentacle attacks + one bite. Can grapple with tentacles (10-ft reach) then bite the grappled target.
Tentacle: 10-ft reach, grapples on hit (DC 13 escape), can restrain if it grapples two tentacles on same target.
Bite: CON DC 15 or diseased. Disease reduces max HP by 1d10 every 24 hours until cured. Lethal over days.
Limited Telepathy: Can communicate simple concepts within 120 ft. It's not mindless — it can bargain.
Otyugh.tactical_identity: Sewer ambusher that lurks in refuse piles, grabs creatures with long tentacles, and bites to infect with disease. Surprisingly intelligent enough (INT 6) to negotiate — it wants food, not fights.
encounter_hooks: disease from Bite reduces max HP daily — even winning the fight has lasting consequences without Lesser Restoration
limited telepathy means it can negotiate — offer it food or garbage and it might let the party pass
lives in refuse — sewer encounters with an otyugh are classic, and the environment is as filthy as the creature
Otyugh.description: A bloated, tentacled creature wallowing in filth, with a vertical mouth full of jagged teeth. It lives in sewers, trash heaps, and dungeon refuse pits — disgusting but sometimes negotiable if you bring it food.
Otyugh.social: Speaks limited telepathy. Can be bribed with refuse and organic waste. Territorial about its garbage heap. May tolerate creatures that feed it.
aftermath_hooks: the otyugh's refuse pile contains objects discarded by dungeon inhabitants — one is valuable or informative
the otyugh's bite carries disease that manifests in days, not hours
the otyugh was placed here deliberately as a waste disposal system — someone maintains this dungeon

Shambling Mound.signature: Lightning Absorption heals it instead of dealing damage — a party using Lightning Bolt actually heals the shambling mound, and its Engulf suffocates grappled creatures.
abilities: Lightning Absorption: Lightning damage heals it instead of dealing damage. Shocking Grasp and Lightning Bolt make it stronger.
Engulf: A grappled Medium or smaller creature is pulled inside. Engulfed creature is blinded, restrained, can't breathe, and takes 2d8+4 bludgeoning at start of each of the mound's turns.
Multiattack: Two slam attacks. If both hit the same target, it's grappled.
Resistance to cold and fire. Immune to lightning (healed by it).
Shambling Mound.tactical_identity: Lumbering engulfer that double-slams to grapple, then pulls victims inside to suffocate. Lightning heals it, cold and fire are halved. The party needs raw physical damage to bring it down.
encounter_hooks: Lightning Absorption means the party's wizard might accidentally heal it — knowledge of the weakness matters
Engulf suffocates — the engulfed character has limited rounds to escape before drowning
in a thunderstorm, a shambling mound is continuously healed by natural lightning — environmental synergy
Shambling Mound.description: A heap of rotting vegetation that moves with slow, crushing force, vaguely humanoid in shape. Lightning makes it stronger, not weaker — a nasty surprise for the party's spellcaster.
aftermath_hooks: the shambling mound's body contains absorbed objects and creatures — something valuable is tangled in the vegetation
lightning in the area feeds the mound — a storm or magical effect created it and may create more
the mound was drawn to a specific magical effect or location — the magic source is still active

Will-o'-Wisp.signature: Consume Life heals it when a nearby creature dies — will-o'-wisps lure victims into danger, wait for them to die, and feed on the death. They CREATE the encounter, not fight it.
abilities: Consume Life: When a creature within 5 ft dies, the wisp feeds and regains 10 HP. It wants creatures to die near it.
Invisibility: At will. The wisp can be invisible whenever it's not attacking.
Shock: 2d8 lightning damage. Small but consistent — the wisp chips away at weakened targets.
Ephemeral: Can't wear/carry anything. Immune to most conditions. AC 19 despite being a light.
Will-o'-Wisp.tactical_identity: Predator that lures victims into natural hazards (quicksand, cliffs, deep water), turns invisible, and waits for them to die. Chips away at weakened creatures with Shock to finish them off, then feeds with Consume Life.
encounter_hooks: the wisp IS the false light — it leads the party into traps, hazards, and ambushes, then feeds on the dead
AC 19 and Invisibility make it extremely hard to kill — but it only has 22 HP, so one solid hit destroys it
in a swamp or dungeon, the 'helpful light ahead' is the encounter — the party follows it into danger
Will-o'-Wisp.description: A flickering ball of ghostly light, easily mistaken for a distant lantern or torch. It lures travelers into danger, then feeds on the life force of the dying — helpful light that leads to death.
Will-o'-Wisp.retreat: Turns invisible and drifts away. Returns when a new victim stumbles into its territory.
aftermath_hooks: the wisp fed on dying creatures in this area — the bodies of its previous victims are scattered nearby
the wisp was drawn to a site of mass death — a battlefield, a plague pit, or a collapsed mine
the wisp's light pattern mimicked a specific landmark or signal — travelers were being led to a specific hazard

Rust Monster.signature: Rust Metal destroys nonmagical metal weapons and armor on contact — a rust monster doesn't threaten HP, it threatens EQUIPMENT. A fighter's plate armor is more at risk than their life.
abilities: Rust Metal: Any nonmagical metal weapon that hits the rust monster corrodes — permanent -1 to damage, destroyed at -5. The fighter's sword degrades with each swing.
Antennae: Touch corrodes nonmagical metal armor — permanent -1 to AC, destroyed at AC 10. The party's plate armor dissolves.
Iron Scent: Detects ferrous metal within 30 ft. It's drawn to the most heavily armored character.
Rust Monster.tactical_identity: Equipment-destroying pest that targets the most metal-heavy character. It doesn't want to fight — it wants to eat metal. The party must find non-metal ways to attack or risk losing their best gear.
encounter_hooks: the threat is EQUIPMENT, not HP — a fighter in full plate is terrified of this CR 1/2 creature
wooden weapons, magic weapons, and spells bypass the rust problem — creative solutions are rewarded
rust monsters can be lured with metal scraps and aren't aggressive — feeding it might be safer than fighting
Rust Monster.description: An insect-like creature with a pair of feathery antennae that corrode metal on contact. Not dangerous to flesh — devastating to equipment. Warriors in plate armor run from this thing.
Rust Monster.retreat: Flees when there's no more metal to consume. Follows the party if they carry metal.
aftermath_hooks: a party member's weapon or armor is corroded — the replacement cost or repair quest becomes a subplot
the rust monster was attracted to a large metal deposit — an ore vein, a cache of weapons, or an iron door it was dissolving
multiple rust monsters in the area means the dungeon's metal features are compromised — doors, locks, and gates may be weakened

Intellect Devourer.signature: Devour Intellect reduces INT to 0 (stunned), then Body Thief steals the body — the party member's brain is consumed and replaced by the intellect devourer controlling their body.
abilities: Devour Intellect: Target makes INT save (DC 12) or takes 2d10 psychic damage. If this reduces INT to 0, the target is stunned until it regains INT. Sets up Body Thief.
Body Thief: Against an incapacitated humanoid, INT contest. If the devourer wins, it teleports inside the creature's skull and consumes the brain. Target dies. The devourer controls the body.
Detect Sentience: Detects thinking creatures within 300 ft. It always knows where the party is.
Intellect Devourer.tactical_identity: Brain parasite that reduces INT with Devour Intellect until the target is stunned, then uses Body Thief to consume the brain and control the body. Tiny and easy to kill — but if it gets inside a party member, that character is dead.
encounter_hooks: Body Thief is instant character death (brain consumed) — one of the most lethal abilities at any CR
a body-thieved NPC looks and acts normal until the party notices something wrong — paranoia encounter
often paired with mind flayers who send intellect devourers ahead as scouts and infiltrators
Intellect Devourer.description: A brain on four legs, small enough to fit in a helmet. It devours a creature's mind and pilots the body like a puppet — the party member who went around the corner comes back... different.
Intellect Devourer.retreat: Flees inside the nearest available body using Body Thief.
aftermath_hooks: the intellect devourer was inhabiting someone the party trusted — that person's body is now brain-dead
it served a mind flayer colony — the devourer was a scout, and its masters know the party exists
the devourer's victim may be recoverable with specific magic — but the window is short

Aboleth.signature: Enslave (WIS DC 14) turns a party member into an obedient puppet — the encounter becomes a fight against your own ally.
abilities: Enslave (3/Day): WIS DC 14 or charmed indefinitely. Target obeys telepathic commands. Only repeats save when harmed — the party must hurt their own friend.
Tentacle: On hit, CON DC 14 or diseased — target can only breathe underwater within 1d4 hours. Creates a post-combat ticking clock.
Mucous Cloud: Any creature touching it underwater must CON save or breathe only water. Punishes melee engagement hard.
Legendary (3/round): Detect (auto-locate all creatures), Tail Swipe (melee + push), Psychic Drain (costs 2 — damages charmed target AND heals itself).
Aboleth.tactical_identity: Paranoid genius that fights from deep water, enslaves a party member first, then lets the party tear itself apart while it drains the charmed victim from safety.
encounter_hooks: lair is always underwater — party must solve breathing and movement before they can even fight
enslaved creatures might include town NPCs or former allies the party recognizes
Probing Telepathy means it knows the party's desires — it can offer deals targeting individual weaknesses
Aboleth.description: A massive, ancient fish-thing with three eyes and tentacles that ooze disease-causing slime. Older than the gods, with perfect memory of every slight against its kind — it enslaves through psychic domination.
Aboleth.retreat: Retreats deeper into water. Its lair actions and regional effects persist. Controls enslaved minions from safety.
Aboleth.social: Telepathic. Offers ancient knowledge in exchange for servitude. Claims to remember the creation of the gods. Every word is manipulation toward enslavement.
aftermath_hooks: the aboleth's Enslave effect persists after combat — a party member or NPC may still be under its control
the aboleth's mucous disease transforms the infected to breathe only water — symptoms manifest over hours
the aboleth remembers everything forever — if it survives, it knows the party intimately and plans accordingly

Acolyte.signature: A 1st-level spellcaster with healing — in groups they keep each other alive, turning what should be an easy fight into a war of attrition.
abilities: Spellcasting: Cure Wounds and Sanctuary are the encounter-relevant spells. Can heal fallen allies or protect a key target from being focused.
Sacred Flame: Ranged cantrip (DEX save) — ignores cover, which matters if the party hides behind obstacles.
Acolyte.tactical_identity: Zealous support caster that heals allies and protects leaders — individually weak but in groups they extend fights significantly by undoing the party's damage.
encounter_hooks: multiple acolytes healing each other forces the party to focus fire or lose the attrition war
religious motivation means they may fight to the death or surrender based on their faith, not self-preservation
often found supporting a stronger leader — killing the acolytes first removes the leader's healing
Acolyte.description: A robed attendant of a temple, carrying a holy symbol and simple weapon. Devoted but not fanatical — more likely to heal and support than to fight.
Acolyte.retreat: Flees to their temple or superior. Calls for divine aid.
Acolyte.social: Devoted to their faith. Helpful if the party's goals align with their deity. Stubborn on matters of doctrine.
aftermath_hooks: the acolyte's temple has resources and contacts the party can access — for a price or service
the acolyte was carrying a message between temples — its contents reveal a religious conflict or conspiracy
the acolyte's holy symbol is from an unfamiliar sect — the faith has a schism the party doesn't know about

Skeleton.signature: Vulnerable to bludgeoning damage — the party's weapon choices matter more than usual, rewarding creative players who grab clubs or throw rocks.
abilities: Shortsword and Shortbow: Melee and ranged options. DEX 14 makes them decent shots — they're more accurate than they look.
Damage vulnerability (bludgeoning): Clubs, hammers, and improvised blunt weapons deal double damage. This IS the tactical hook.
Immunities: Poison damage and exhaustion do nothing. Condition immune to poisoned and exhaustion — attrition doesn't work.
Skeleton.tactical_identity: Mindless automaton that attacks the nearest moving thing. No tactics, no fear, no retreat. Fights until destroyed. Dangerous in numbers because they never break.
encounter_hooks: bludgeoning vulnerability rewards players who adapt — grabbing a chair leg or kicking matters
undead that never flee or negotiate — the fight always goes to the last skeleton standing
can wield any weapon they're given — a skeleton with a greatsword is the same CR but tells a different story
Skeleton.description: Animated bones held together by dark magic, moving with a jerky, mechanical gait. No intelligence, no fear, no pain — it follows orders until destroyed.
aftermath_hooks: the skeletons wear armor or carry weapons from a recognizable military unit — they were soldiers once
one skeleton clutches a locket or letter that identifies who it was in life
the animating magic lingers — if the source isn't found, more skeletons will rise

Zombie.signature: Undead Fortitude lets it survive killing blows — a zombie that drops to 0 HP makes a CON save to pop back up with 1 HP, turning 'dead' enemies into ongoing threats.
abilities: Undead Fortitude: On dropping to 0 HP, CON save (DC = 5 + damage taken). Success means it gets back up with 1 HP. Radiant damage and crits bypass this.
Slam: Basic melee attack. Low damage but the threat is volume and persistence, not individual hits.
Zombie.tactical_identity: Relentless meat wall that keeps getting back up. No intelligence, no tactics — just shambles toward the nearest living thing and refuses to stay dead.
encounter_hooks: Undead Fortitude makes the party waste actions re-killing them unless they use radiant damage or crits
slow speed (20 ft) means the party can kite them — but only if they have room to retreat
work best as a rising tide that keeps coming while something worse happens elsewhere
Zombie.description: A shambling corpse with dead eyes and grasping hands, smelling of rot and grave dirt. Slow but relentless, and disturbingly hard to put down — it keeps getting back up.
aftermath_hooks: the zombies' clothing and gear identify them as people from a nearby settlement — someone the locals knew
the necromantic source is still active — anything that dies here may rise within hours
one zombie carries a holy symbol of a good-aligned god — a cleric fell here

Goblin.signature: Nimble Escape lets it Hide or Disengage as a bonus action every turn — goblins vanish after attacking and reappear from new positions, making them maddening to pin down.
abilities: Nimble Escape: Hide or Disengage as bonus action. This IS goblin combat — attack, vanish, reappear somewhere else. The party chases ghosts.
Scimitar and Shortbow: Melee and ranged options. Low damage individually but in groups with Nimble Escape they chip away while staying safe.
Goblin.tactical_identity: Cowardly hit-and-run skirmisher that attacks from hiding, uses Nimble Escape to vanish, and repeats from a new position. Never stands and fights — always running, always hiding.
encounter_hooks: pre-prepared ambush terrain with hiding spots is what makes goblins deadly — open ground neutralizes them
Nimble Escape means Opportunity Attacks don't work — the party needs ranged attacks or area spells
a smart leader (bugbear, hobgoblin) directing goblins elevates them from nuisance to genuine threat
Goblin.description: A small, green-skinned creature with oversized ears and a cruel grin, barely three feet tall. Cowardly alone, dangerous in groups — cunning enough to set ambushes and traps.
Goblin.retreat: Flees immediately when outmatched. Screams warnings to alert nearby allies.
Goblin.social: Grovels, lies, and bargains when cornered. Offers to betray its boss for survival. Information is unreliable but occasionally true.
aftermath_hooks: a crude map scratched on leather showing nearby lair entrances and a symbol the party doesn't recognize
a stolen item from a local settlement — the owner will want it back and may have a story
the goblins were fleeing something deeper in the cave — what drove them out is still in there

Bugbear.signature: Surprise Attack adds 2d6 damage on the first hit against a surprised creature — bugbears are built to ambush, and the encounter should start with them already hidden.
abilities: Surprise Attack: Extra 2d6 damage if the target hasn't acted yet. The entire encounter design should guarantee the bugbear gets this off.
Brute: Melee weapons deal one extra damage die. A morningstar hit does 2d8+2 instead of 1d8+2 — bugbears hit much harder than their CR suggests.
Morningstar and Javelin: Strong melee with a ranged backup. Javelin for targets that stay at distance.
Bugbear.tactical_identity: Patient ambush predator that hides, waits for the perfect moment, and opens with devastating Surprise Attack damage before switching to sustained Brute-enhanced melee.
encounter_hooks: Surprise Attack means encounter design must give the bugbear a hiding spot and a reason to wait — it should strike first
Brute makes its damage output rival CR 3+ creatures — a bugbear opening hit can drop a level 1 character
often leads goblins — the bugbear hides while goblins bait the party into the ambush zone
Bugbear.description: A hulking, furry goblinoid that moves with surprising stealth for its size. Lazy and brutal — it prefers ambush because fighting is less work than chasing.
Bugbear.retreat: Flees if ambush fails and it takes serious damage. Seeks revenge later from a better position.
Bugbear.social: Intimidates and bullies. Demands food, coin, or servitude. Respects only physical threat.
aftermath_hooks: trophies taken from previous victims — a signet ring, a holy symbol, a child's toy
the bugbear was posted here by someone else — scratched orders indicate a goblinoid hierarchy nearby
its ambush point overlooks a trade route or path — this spot was chosen deliberately

Wolf.signature: Pack Tactics gives advantage on attacks when an ally is adjacent — a single wolf is trivial, but a pack shreds targets through guaranteed advantage.
abilities: Pack Tactics: Advantage on attacks if an ally is within 5 ft of the target. Two wolves flanking the same target rarely miss.
Bite: On hit, STR DC 11 or target is knocked prone. A prone target gives advantage to all melee — the pack piles on.
Keen Hearing and Smell: Advantage on Perception checks. Wolves always detect the party first — surprise favors them.
Wolf.tactical_identity: Pack hunter that circles prey with allies to trigger Pack Tactics, knocks targets prone with Bite, then the whole pack piles on the downed creature. Flees when outnumbered or when the alpha falls.
encounter_hooks: Pack Tactics means killing even one wolf reduces the pack's accuracy significantly — focus fire works
prone from Bite + Pack Tactics advantage creates a death spiral for isolated characters
wolves almost always detect the party first — the encounter starts on their terms, not the party's
Wolf.description: A gray-furred predator with yellow eyes, lean and watchful. Hunts in coordinated packs — when you see one wolf, the others are already surrounding you.
Wolf.retreat: Flees when pack alpha falls or pack is reduced to one.
aftermath_hooks: the wolves' den contains bones and gear from previous victims — one item is identifiable
the pack was unusually aggressive — something drove them from their normal territory
wolf pups remain in the den — a druid or ranger NPC might care about their fate

Dire Wolf.signature: Pack Tactics plus a powerful bite that knocks prone — a dire wolf leading regular wolves creates a devastating knock-down-and-pile-on combo.
abilities: Pack Tactics: Advantage on attacks when an ally is adjacent. Same as regular wolves but with much higher damage.
Bite: +5 to hit, 2d6+3 damage, STR DC 13 or knocked prone. Harder save than regular wolves and the damage is serious.
Keen Hearing and Smell: Always detects the party first.
Dire Wolf.tactical_identity: Alpha predator that leads a wolf pack — charges the most isolated target, knocks it prone, and the rest of the pack swarms in with Pack Tactics advantage on the downed creature.
encounter_hooks: leading a wolf pack is the classic encounter — the dire wolf picks the target, the wolves finish it
STR DC 13 to avoid prone means even armored characters get knocked down regularly
large size means it can grapple medium creatures and drag them away from the group
Dire Wolf.description: A wolf the size of a horse, with jaws that can drag a grown man to the ground. Smarter and more aggressive than normal wolves — often used as mounts by orcs and goblins.
Dire Wolf.retreat: Flees when badly wounded, dragging a carcass or packmate if possible.
aftermath_hooks: the dire wolf's territory marks overlap with a much larger predator's — something bigger lives nearby
its den contains remains of livestock with a brand — a nearby farm has been losing animals
the wolf's size and aggression suggest magical influence — its fur has an unnatural sheen or color

Kobold.signature: Pack Tactics gives advantage when an ally is adjacent, but Sunlight Sensitivity gives disadvantage in sunlight — kobolds are terrifying underground and pathetic outside.
abilities: Pack Tactics: Advantage on attacks with an ally nearby. Cancels out Sunlight Sensitivity in daylight — but underground, it's pure advantage.
Sunlight Sensitivity: Disadvantage on attacks and Perception in direct sunlight. Encounter location is everything for kobolds.
Dagger and Sling: Low damage but Pack Tactics means they hit often. Volume of attacks is their weapon.
Kobold.tactical_identity: Trap-building swarm fighter that overwhelms through numbers and Pack Tactics in dark, prepared environments. Individually pathetic but their lairs are engineered death traps.
encounter_hooks: kobold lairs are the encounter — the creatures are incidental to the traps, murder holes, and collapsing tunnels they've built
Sunlight Sensitivity means forcing them outside or casting Light fundamentally changes the fight
often serve a dragon — kobold encounters can foreshadow a much larger threat
Kobold.description: A tiny, reptilian humanoid with a yapping voice and an obsessive devotion to traps and dragons. Pathetic alone — in their lair, surrounded by traps they built, they are lethal.
Kobold.retreat: Flees through pre-planned escape tunnels too small for medium creatures. Regroups and resets traps.
Kobold.social: Serves the strongest creature nearby. Offers servitude or information to avoid death. Will immediately betray the party to its true master.
aftermath_hooks: the trap components reveal surprising engineering — someone taught these kobolds, or they stole blueprints
a tunnel network with multiple exits — the kobolds can appear anywhere in the area
a shrine to a dragon, real or imagined — the kobolds believe they serve a draconic master

Orc.signature: Aggressive lets it move its full speed toward a hostile creature as a bonus action — orcs close distance terrifyingly fast, denying ranged characters their safety.
abilities: Aggressive: Bonus action to move full speed toward a visible enemy. An orc 60 feet away is in melee range this turn. Ranged characters can't kite.
Greataxe: 1d12+3 damage. Heavy single hits that threaten squishy characters.
Javelin: Ranged option for when they can't close yet, but they always prefer to charge.
Orc.tactical_identity: Bloodthirsty charger that uses Aggressive to close gaps instantly and overwhelm ranged characters. No patience, no subtlety — just a straight-line sprint into melee.
encounter_hooks: Aggressive makes distance meaningless — archers and spellcasters get one round before orcs are on them
multiple orcs using Aggressive simultaneously turns a 'ranged encounter' into a melee brawl instantly
orcs are aggressive but not stupid (INT 7) — they target whoever looks weakest, not whoever is closest
Orc.description: A muscular, gray-green humanoid with a jutting jaw and tusks, built for war. Aggressive and direct — orcs charge first and think never.
Orc.retreat: Retreats only when a leader falls. Individual orcs fight to the death if cornered.
Orc.social: Respects strength above all. A defeated orc may swear temporary allegiance. Challenges to single combat are honored.
aftermath_hooks: a crude shrine to Gruumsh with offerings that reveal what the warband prays for — victory, vengeance, or a specific target
a captive from a nearby settlement held for ransom or sport
the warband's trail leads back to a much larger encampment — this was a scouting party

Bandit.signature: No special abilities — bandits are defined by their motivation, not their statblock. They want money, not martyrdom, and will surrender or flee when the fight turns.
abilities: Scimitar and Light Crossbow: Basic melee and ranged. Nothing special — the encounter interest comes from who they are and what they want.
No special traits: Bandits are blank slates. Their tactical interest is entirely situational — numbers, terrain, ambush, hostages.
Bandit.tactical_identity: Self-interested criminal who fights only when the odds favor them. Surrenders when bloodied, flees when outmatched, and negotiates when cornered. Never fights to the death.
encounter_hooks: bandits negotiate, bluff, and surrender — unlike monsters, combat is optional and the party has social tools
always fight with an advantage — ambush, numbers, terrain, hostages — or they wouldn't fight at all
capturing bandits creates roleplaying opportunities — interrogation, moral choices, turning them over to law
Bandit.description: A rough-looking human in mismatched armor, desperate enough to rob travelers. More interested in gold than glory — most will flee or surrender when outmatched.
Bandit.retreat: Flees when half the group falls. Scatters in different directions.
Bandit.social: Demands coin or goods. Willing to negotiate if outnumbered. Some may be desperate commoners, not career criminals.
aftermath_hooks: stolen goods bearing merchant marks that could be returned for reward or used to track the victims
a wanted poster for one of the bandits — someone is offering a bounty
the camp shows signs of desperation — these bandits were displaced by something worse

Bandit Captain.signature: Three attacks per turn (two scimitars + dagger) with a Parry reaction that adds 2 AC — a surprisingly dangerous martial combatant who can rival a party fighter in output.
abilities: Multiattack: Two scimitar attacks + one dagger. Three attacks per turn at CR 2 is unusually high action economy.
Parry: Reaction to add +2 AC against one melee attack. Makes them slippery in duels — the party fighter can't just trade blows.
Scimitar and Dagger: Combined damage per round is solid. The captain genuinely threatens party members.
Bandit Captain.tactical_identity: Cunning leader who lets bandits soften the party, then engages the weakest-looking target personally. Uses Parry to survive focus fire and calls retreat when the fight is clearly lost.
encounter_hooks: killing or capturing the captain usually ends the fight — the bandits lose their nerve without leadership
three attacks per round makes the captain a genuine melee threat that players don't expect from CR 2
a captain with a name and a motive elevates a generic bandit encounter into a recurring villain
Bandit Captain.description: A scarred, confident figure who commands through a mix of charisma and intimidation. Smarter and tougher than the rabble — the one giving orders while the others do the dying.
Bandit Captain.retreat: Surrenders when outmatched, offering ransom or information. Or flees and sends word to allies for revenge.
Bandit Captain.social: Negotiates from strength — demands toll, ransom, or service. Respects competence. May hire the party if they prove capable.
aftermath_hooks: a ledger of protection payments from local merchants — the bandits were running a business, not random raids
a sealed letter from someone in authority — the bandits had a patron in the nearest town
one bandit is a deserter from a military unit — their former commander wants them back, dead or alive

Hobgoblin.signature: Martial Advantage adds 2d6 damage once per turn when an ally is adjacent — hobgoblins reward disciplined formations and punish parties that let themselves get flanked.
abilities: Martial Advantage: Extra 2d6 damage once per turn if an ally is within 5 ft of the target. Similar to Pack Tactics but damage-focused instead of accuracy-focused.
Longsword and Longbow: Proper military weapons with good range. Hobgoblins fight like soldiers, not savages.
Hobgoblin.tactical_identity: Disciplined soldier that fights in formation to trigger Martial Advantage. Holds shield walls, provides covering fire, and retreats in good order when commanders fall.
encounter_hooks: Martial Advantage means breaking their formation is the key — isolated hobgoblins lose their damage bonus
military discipline means they follow orders, hold positions, and don't break easily — very different from goblins
often found in organized war camps with sentries, patrols, and fortifications — not random encounters
Hobgoblin.description: A disciplined, armored goblinoid standing taller than a human, with flat features and orange-red skin. Militaristic and organized — hobgoblins fight in formation and follow orders without question.
Hobgoblin.retreat: Falls back in formation to a defensible position. Does not rout — retreats with discipline.
Hobgoblin.social: Demands surrender or tribute. Respects demonstrated strength. May take prisoners for labor or ransom.
aftermath_hooks: orders or dispatches revealing troop movements and a larger military objective
a prisoner taken for interrogation — still alive, knows something the party needs
a war banner or insignia identifying which legion this unit belongs to

Gnoll.signature: Rampage lets it make a bonus Bite attack after reducing a creature to 0 HP — gnolls snowball when they start killing, getting faster and more dangerous with each victim.
abilities: Rampage: When it reduces a creature to 0 HP, bonus action to move half speed and Bite. Gnolls chain kills — dropping one target feeds into attacking the next.
Bite, Spear, and Longbow: Versatile loadout. The spear is their main weapon, but Rampage Bite is what makes them terrifying.
Gnoll.tactical_identity: Frenzied pack hunter that escalates as bodies drop. Targets the weakest creature to trigger Rampage, then chains bonus attacks through multiple victims in a single turn.
encounter_hooks: Rampage means low-HP allies (horses, NPCs, pets) are liabilities — gnolls kill them to fuel bonus attacks into the party
bringing hirelings or escorting civilians into gnoll territory is extremely dangerous for this reason
gnolls don't negotiate or surrender — they're demon-driven. Every gnoll fight is to the death
Gnoll.description: A hyena-headed humanoid, hunched and cackling, reeking of carrion and madness. Driven by an insatiable demonic hunger — gnolls don't negotiate, they devour.
Gnoll.retreat: Does not retreat — driven by demonic hunger. Fights until destroyed.
aftermath_hooks: the carnage trail leads to a destroyed caravan or farmstead — survivors may be hiding nearby
a crude fetish to Yeenoghu marks this pack's territory — the demon lord's influence is spreading
the gnolls were heading somewhere specific — their path points toward a settlement

Ghoul.signature: Claws paralyze on a failed CON save — a paralyzed character auto-fails STR/DEX saves, attacks against them auto-crit, and they can't act. One failed save can end a character.
abilities: Claws: CON DC 10 or paralyzed for 1 minute (repeat save each turn). Paralyzed = auto-crit in melee. Two ghouls on a paralyzed target deal devastating damage.
Bite: Bonus damage on a paralyzed or incapacitated target. The combo is Claws to paralyze, then Bite for extra damage.
Elves are immune to ghoul paralysis — an elf in the party changes the encounter dynamic significantly.
Ghoul.tactical_identity: Pack predator that paralyzes one target with Claws, then the whole group feeds on the helpless victim. Individually fragile but paralysis makes them punch far above their CR.
encounter_hooks: one failed CON save against paralysis can spiral into a character death — ghouls are more lethal than their CR suggests
multiple ghouls means multiple paralysis attempts per round — someone will eventually fail
elf party members are immune to the paralysis — this is a rare case where race choice directly impacts encounter difficulty
Ghoul.description: A hunched, gray-skinned creature with elongated claws and a mouth full of jagged teeth, reeking of the grave. Once humanoid, now driven by an endless hunger for flesh — its touch paralyzes the living.
aftermath_hooks: the ghouls were feeding on something specific — the remains identify a missing person or a massacred group
ghoul tunnels lead to a larger underground network — the ghouls were the outermost symptom of something deeper
one ghoul wears the vestments of a priest or healer — it was turned recently and its congregation doesn't know

Ghast.signature: Stench forces CON saves on nearby creatures or they're poisoned — combined with ghoul-style paralysis, ghasts create a debuff zone that weakens the party before claws even land.
abilities: Stench: Any creature starting its turn within 5 ft makes CON DC 10 or is poisoned until its next turn. Poisoned = disadvantage on attacks and ability checks.
Claws: CON DC 10 or paralyzed for 1 minute. Same as ghouls but ghasts can also paralyze elves.
Turning Defiance: The ghast and nearby ghouls have advantage on saves against Turn Undead. Clerics can't easily dismiss a ghast-led pack.
Ghast.tactical_identity: Alpha ghoul that poisons nearby enemies with Stench while paralyzing with Claws. Leading a pack of regular ghouls, it makes Turn Undead harder and covers the paralysis gap against elves.
encounter_hooks: Stench + paralysis means melee characters face poisoned disadvantage AND paralysis risk simultaneously
Turning Defiance protects nearby ghouls — kill the ghast first or Turn Undead keeps failing
ghasts can paralyze elves unlike regular ghouls — the elf's immunity no longer applies
Ghast.description: A ghoul that retains a flicker of its former intelligence, surrounded by a nauseating stench. It leads packs of ghouls and can paralyze even elves — smarter, stronger, and fouler than its lesser kin.
Ghast.retreat: Flees if isolated from its ghoul pack. Returns with reinforcements.
aftermath_hooks: the ghast retains enough intelligence to have been leading the ghouls deliberately — its lair shows planning
a holy symbol or military insignia on the ghast identifies who it was before turning
the stench lingers in the area for days — locals will know something died here

Shadow.signature: Strength Drain permanently reduces STR on hit — reaching 0 STR kills the target and creates a new shadow. Fights snowball as the party weakens and fallen allies rise as enemies.
abilities: Strength Drain: On hit, reduces target's STR by 1d4. This doesn't recover after the fight without Greater Restoration. At STR 0, the target dies and becomes a shadow.
Amorphous: Can squeeze through 1-inch gaps. Can attack from inside walls, under doors, through cracks.
Shadow Stealth: In dim light or darkness, can Hide as a bonus action. Nearly invisible in dark environments.
Sunlight Weakness: 1 damage per round in sunlight and disadvantage on everything. Light is the hard counter.
Shadow.tactical_identity: Assassin that hides in darkness, drains STR with every hit, and creates new shadows from its kills. Avoids light at all costs — fights by ambushing from shadows and retreating into walls.
encounter_hooks: STR drain deaths create new shadows — a TPK can snowball from one character falling
light sources become critical survival tools, not just convenience — losing your torch mid-fight is terrifying
Amorphous means there's nowhere to hide — shadows come through walls, floors, and cracks
Shadow.description: A dark, featureless silhouette that moves independently of any source, draining the strength from living creatures with a cold touch. Barely sentient — drawn to warmth and life like a moth to flame.
aftermath_hooks: the shadows were tethered to something — a cursed object, a murder site, or an unburied body
a creature killed by Strength Drain rises as a new shadow in 1d4 hours — a fallen NPC will return as an enemy
the temperature returns to normal once the shadows are destroyed — frost patterns on the walls outline a hidden message

Specter.signature: Life Drain reduces max HP on hit — the party's health ceiling drops with every successful attack, and it doesn't come back until a long rest.
abilities: Life Drain: Necrotic damage AND max HP reduced by the damage dealt. Can't be healed above the new max. At 0 max HP, the target dies.
Incorporeal Movement: Moves through creatures and objects. Can attack from inside walls and retreat into solid matter.
Sunlight Sensitivity: Disadvantage on attacks in sunlight. Like shadows, specters are darkness creatures.
Specter.tactical_identity: Hit-and-fade attacker that phases through walls to strike, drains max HP, then retreats into solid matter where the party can't follow. Wears the party down over multiple engagements.
encounter_hooks: max HP reduction makes every hit permanently weaken the party — healing spells can't fix it
incorporeal movement means walls don't protect the party — specters choose when and where to engage
multiple specters in a haunted location create a war of attrition the party can't win without dealing with the source
Specter.description: A translucent figure that sucks warmth from the air — breath fogs, torches dim. The remnant of a person who died violently, now driven by hatred of the living.
aftermath_hooks: the specter was created by a more powerful undead — a wraith, vampire, or lich controls it
the specter haunts a specific spot where it died — the cause of death reveals a murder or betrayal
Life Drain permanently reduces max HP — a party member may need Greater Restoration before the next fight

Wight.signature: Life Drain reduces max HP AND can kill at 0 max HP, turning the victim into a zombie under the wight's command — it builds an army from its kills.
abilities: Life Drain: Necrotic damage, reduces max HP, and if this kills the target, it rises as a zombie under the wight's control 24 hours later.
