Violet Fungus.signature: A fungus that makes 1d4 attacks per turn with 10 ft. reach and necrotic damage — looks harmless until it hits 4 times in one round for up to 16 necrotic.
abilities: Multiattack: 1d4 Rotting Touch attacks per turn.
Rotting Touch: +2 to hit, reach 10 ft., 4 (1d8) necrotic.
False Appearance: Looks like ordinary fungus. Blindsight 30 ft. Speed 5 ft. AC 5.
Violet Fungus.tactical_identity: Looks like a normal mushroom. When anything comes within 10 ft., it lashes out with 1-4 necrotic attacks. On a good roll (4 attacks), that's 16 necrotic damage in one turn from a CR 1/4 creature — potentially lethal to level 1 characters. AC 5 and 18 HP mean it dies instantly to any focused attack, but the surprise damage is already dealt. Paired with shriekers, the shrieker alerts while the violet fungus kills.
encounter_hooks: 1d4 attacks means damage is wildly variable — sometimes it hits once for 4, sometimes four times for 16, creating unpredictable danger
10 ft. reach means it attacks characters who think they're at safe distance — the tentacles are longer than expected
Paired with shriekers: the shrieker screams, the party investigates, and walks into violet fungus reach — a classic Underdark trap combo
Violet Fungus.description: A four-foot-tall purple mushroom with tentacles that whip out to rot living flesh. It doesn't chase — it stands perfectly still in the dark until something walks within reach.
aftermath_hooks: the fungus grew around something it was slowly consuming — the partially dissolved object is identifiable
the necrotic damage from its touch has contaminated the soil — nothing healthy will grow here for years
the fungus colony marks a boundary — beyond it, the underdark ecology changes significantly

Vulture.signature: Pack Tactics scavenger with Keen Sight and Smell — the aerial version of the hyena, circling overhead and descending to feed on the weak.
abilities: Pack Tactics: Advantage on attacks if ally within 5 ft.
Keen Sight and Smell: Advantage on sight/smell Perception.
Beak: +2 to hit, 2 (1d4) piercing. Fly 50 ft.
Vulture.tactical_identity: Circles at altitude waiting for something to die or weaken. Pack Tactics in a flock gives advantage on every beak attack, but +2 to hit and 2 damage mean they're no threat to adventurers. They're scavengers that feed on the aftermath. Their tactical role is atmosphere and tracking — vultures circling ahead means something died recently.
encounter_hooks: Vultures circling a location is a visual signal that something died — a scouting clue visible from miles away
A flock descending on a wounded NPC creates time pressure — reach the victim before the vultures finish the job
Vultures that attack living creatures suggest unnatural hunger, corruption, or a druid commanding them
Vulture.description: A large scavenging bird that circles dying creatures with unsettling patience. Where vultures gather, something below is dead or dying.
aftermath_hooks: the vultures were circling something dead — the body tells a story
the flock indicates recent death in the area — a battlefield, a plague, or a predator
vultures following the party means something thinks they're going to die

Warhorse.signature: The cavalry mount — Trampling Charge with DC 14 prone and a bonus-action hooves attack for 22 total damage on a charge, plus 60 ft. speed for hit-and-run lance charges.
abilities: Trampling Charge: Move 20+ ft. straight, hit with Hooves, DC 14 STR or prone. If prone, another Hooves as bonus action.
Hooves: +6 to hit, 11 (2d6+4) bludgeoning.
Speed 60 ft.
Warhorse.tactical_identity: THE mounted combat beast. Charges 60 ft., attacks with hooves for 11 damage, DC 14 knocks prone, bonus-action hooves for another 11 — 22 damage from the mount alone, plus the rider's lance charge. A mounted knight on a warhorse deals combined 30+ damage on a charge. Trained for war, it doesn't panic in combat. The warhorse makes cavalry encounters work mechanically.
encounter_hooks: A mounted knight charging on a warhorse combines mount damage (22) with rider damage (lance + charge) for devastating first-round impact
DC 14 Trampling Charge from every mount in a cavalry line means most infantry goes prone — historically accurate and mechanically brutal
Warhorses are expensive and valuable — stealing or killing enemy warhorses before a battle is a legitimate strategic objective
Warhorse.description: A large, trained destrier that charges into battle without flinching. A true combat mount — its trampling charge is devastating and it doesn't spook.
Warhorse.retreat: Gallops away. Trained to return to its stable or last camp.
aftermath_hooks: the warhorse's barding and tack identify its rider — military unit, noble house, or knightly order
the horse was separated from its rider during a battle or ambush — the rider is missing nearby
the warhorse is trained and valuable — capturing it alive is worth significant gold

Warhorse Skeleton.signature: An undead mount that never tires, never panics, and never needs to eat — bludgeoning vulnerability is the tradeoff for a fearless cavalry skeleton with 60 ft. speed.
abilities: Hooves: +6 to hit, 11 (2d6+4) bludgeoning.
Speed 60 ft. Bludgeoning vulnerability. Poison immunity. Exhaustion immunity.
Darkvision 60 ft. AC 13.
Warhorse Skeleton.tactical_identity: A tireless undead mount. No Trampling Charge (the living warhorse has it, the skeleton doesn't), so it's mechanically simpler — just hooves. Its value is as a necromancer's mount: it doesn't eat, doesn't sleep, doesn't tire, doesn't panic, and rides through the night without exhaustion. Bludgeoning vulnerability means warhammers and maces wreck it fast. It charges where commanded without self-preservation.
encounter_hooks: A death knight or necromancer on a warhorse skeleton rides without rest, pursues without tiring, and doesn't need to stop for the mount — relentless pursuit
Bludgeoning vulnerability means a cleric with a mace is the ideal answer — turning or smashing, either works
Skeletal cavalry charging out of fog or darkness is an iconic horror encounter — the horses are silent, the riders are dead, and the charge doesn't stop
Warhorse Skeleton.description: The animated skeleton of a warhorse, tireless and obedient, with bone hooves clattering on stone. An undead mount that never tires, never eats, and never fears — but vulnerable to bludgeoning.
aftermath_hooks: the skeleton still bears the barding of a specific military order — the undead army was once a real one
the skeleton served a death knight or undead cavalry — its rider is nearby
the animating magic on the horse is the same as on nearby humanoid undead — one necromancer raised them all

Weasel.signature: Tiny beast with Keen Hearing and Smell and Stealth +5 — a familiar and scout option that detects hidden things better than most adventurers.
abilities: Keen Hearing and Smell: Advantage on hearing/smell Perception.
Bite: +5 to hit, 1 piercing.
Stealth +5. Perception +3. Speed 30 ft.
Weasel.tactical_identity: A scouting familiar, not a combatant. Stealth +5 with Keen Hearing and Smell makes it one of the best mundane tracker-scouts at CR 0. It sneaks into small spaces, detects hidden creatures by smell, and reports back (if a familiar). +5 to hit is absurdly high for CR 0 but deals only 1 damage.
encounter_hooks: As a familiar, Keen senses with advantage cover the two most common detection types — hearing and smell — making it a reliable early warning system
A weasel's small size lets it scout through walls, pipes, and gaps too small for other creatures — perfect for dungeon reconnaissance
A weasel behaving oddly (approaching the party, leading them somewhere) might be a druid's companion or a polymorphed creature with a message
Weasel.description: A tiny, slender predator with keen hearing and smell. An effective familiar for its Perception — small enough to go anywhere unnoticed.
Weasel.retreat: Darts into a burrow or gap.
aftermath_hooks: the weasel was a familiar — its master is aware
the weasel's burrow contains small stolen objects — a key, a button, a tooth
the weasel's behavior was unusual — it was watching the party deliberately

Werebear.signature: The tankiest lycanthrope at 135 HP with nonmagical B/P/S immunity — a neutral good shapechanger whose bite spreads lycanthropy as a blessing rather than a curse.
abilities: Multiattack: Two Claw attacks (bear form) or two Greataxe attacks (humanoid). Hybrid can use either.
Bite (bear/hybrid): +7 to hit, 15 (2d10+4) piercing. DC 14 CON or cursed with werebear lycanthropy.
Claw (bear/hybrid): +7 to hit, 13 (2d8+4) slashing.
Greataxe (humanoid/hybrid): +7 to hit, 10 (1d12+4) slashing.
Immunity: B/P/S from nonmagical, non-silvered attacks. 135 HP. Keen Smell. Climb 30 ft. in bear/hybrid.
Werebear.tactical_identity: A walking fortress. 135 HP with nonmagical weapon immunity means parties without silvered or magic weapons literally cannot hurt it. Two claws for 26 average damage per round is solid, and the bite adds lycanthropy transmission. As neutral good, werebears are more likely allies than enemies — they're forest protectors and hermit guardians. In combat, they shift to hybrid form for maximum versatility, absorb damage, and maul whoever threatens their charge.
encounter_hooks: Nonmagical weapon immunity at CR 5 is a hard wall for unprepared parties — this encounter demands silver weapons, magic, or diplomacy
As a neutral good lycanthrope, the werebear is the rare monster that might deliberately bite a willing PC to grant them lycanthropy as a gift
A werebear hermit protecting a forest shrine is a guardian NPC, not a villain — the party must prove their intentions before being allowed past
Werebear.description: In hybrid form, a massive humanoid covered in thick brown fur with a bear's head and claws. The most benevolent lycanthrope — often serves as a forest protector, only aggressive when the wild is threatened.
Werebear.retreat: Withdraws into deep wilderness. Only fights when its territory or charges are threatened.
Werebear.social: Good-natured and protective. May share the lycanthropy curse voluntarily with a worthy individual. Defends the weak and innocent.
aftermath_hooks: the werebear was protecting a community or group of travelers — they now lack their guardian
the werebear can voluntarily transmit its curse as a gift — a party member may face this choice
the werebear's territory contained a natural wonder or healing spring it was guarding from exploitation

Wereboar.signature: A charging lycanthrope with Relentless — it won't go down on the first kill shot, and its tusk-bite spreads lycanthropy on every hit in boar or hybrid form.
abilities: Multiattack (humanoid/hybrid): Two attacks, only one with Tusks.
Tusks (boar/hybrid): +5 to hit, 10 (2d6+3) slashing. DC 12 CON or cursed with wereboar lycanthropy.
Maul (humanoid/hybrid): +5 to hit, 10 (2d6+3) bludgeoning.
Charge (boar/hybrid): Move 15+ ft. straight, hit with Tusks, +7 (2d6) extra damage. DC 13 STR or prone.
Relentless (1/short rest): If 14 damage or less would drop it to 0, drops to 1 HP instead.
Immunity: B/P/S from nonmagical, non-silvered attacks. 78 HP.
Wereboar.tactical_identity: Charges in boar or hybrid form for 17 damage on the tusks with a prone knockdown, then follows up with a maul on the downed target. Relentless means it survives one killing blow (under 14 damage), getting a second wind that catches parties off guard. Nonmagical weapon immunity is the real threat — without silver or magic, the party bounces off. Wereboars are bullies and bandits, fighting in gangs and spreading their curse through aggressive tusk attacks.
encounter_hooks: Relentless on top of nonmagical immunity means the wereboar refuses to die twice — once from immunity, once from the trait itself
Lycanthropy spread through tusks means every melee hit is a potential curse — the wereboar doesn't need to win the fight to ruin someone's week
A bandit gang that turns into boar-hybrids mid-fight transforms a routine encounter into a lycanthrope crisis
Wereboar.description: In hybrid form, a stocky, bristle-covered humanoid with tusks and a pig's snout. Ill-tempered and territorial — charges headlong into battle and keeps fighting through wounds that should stop it.
Wereboar.retreat: Charges through obstacles to escape. Does not sneak — barrels through walls if necessary.
Wereboar.social: Aggressive but territorial rather than predatory. Demands to be left alone. Defends its homestead or herd with lethal force.
aftermath_hooks: the wereboar was protecting a family or farmstead — they know what it was and depended on its protection
the curse spreads through its gore attacks — anyone gored must be checked for infection
the wereboar had been feuding with local hunters — both sides have a version of the story

Wererat.signature: The sneaky, urban lycanthrope — low damage but nonmagical weapon immunity, Stealth +4, and a sewer-dwelling criminal network that fights with crossbows and ambushes rather than brute force.
abilities: Multiattack (humanoid/hybrid): Two attacks, only one Bite.
Bite (rat/hybrid): +4 to hit, 4 (1d4+2) piercing. DC 11 CON or cursed with wererat lycanthropy.
Shortsword (humanoid/hybrid): +4 to hit, 5 (1d6+2) piercing.
Hand Crossbow (humanoid/hybrid): +4 to hit, range 30/120, 5 (1d6+2) piercing.
Immunity: B/P/S from nonmagical, non-silvered attacks. Keen Smell. Stealth +4. Darkvision 60 ft.
Wererat.tactical_identity: Fights dirty from the sewers. Opens with hand crossbow bolts from hiding, then closes to melee in hybrid form where nonmagical weapon immunity makes it nearly invincible to most city guards. Low damage (5 per hit) means it's not a brute — it's a survivor. In groups, wererats surround targets in tunnels, bite to spread lycanthropy, and retreat into the sewers when pressed. They're organized criminals, not wild beasts — they negotiate, blackmail, and assassinate.
encounter_hooks: Nonmagical weapon immunity means city guards can't fight wererats — the party is called in specifically because normal soldiers are useless
A wererat thieves' guild operates from the sewers with escape routes into rat form through tiny drains — they're nearly impossible to corner
Lycanthropy at DC 11 is low, but every bite is a chance — a wererat gang that bites and retreats is spreading the curse without needing to win fights
Wererat.description: In hybrid form, a hunched, twitchy humanoid with a rat's face and naked tail. An urban predator that runs in criminal gangs — more likely to ambush from sewer tunnels than fight openly.
Wererat.retreat: Reverts to rat form and flees through sewer grates or wall cracks. Impossible to pursue in rat form.
Wererat.social: In humanoid form, operates as a thief or informant. Bargains with secrets and stolen goods. Serves a thieves' guild or pack leader.
aftermath_hooks: the wererat was part of an underground network — sewer tunnels lead to a thieves' den or smuggling operation
the wererat had been stealing something specific and regularly — its stash reveals the pattern
a bitten victim doesn't know they're infected — the curse manifests at the worst possible time

Weretiger.signature: The most versatile lycanthrope — Pounce knockdown in tiger/hybrid form, longbow at range in humanoid form, 120 HP with nonmagical immunity, and Stealth +4 for ambush tactics.
abilities: Multiattack (humanoid/hybrid): Two Scimitar or two Longbow attacks in humanoid; two Claw attacks in hybrid.
Pounce (tiger/hybrid): Move 15+ ft. straight, hit with Claw, DC 14 STR or prone. If prone, Bite as bonus action.
Bite (tiger/hybrid): +5 to hit, 8 (1d10+3) piercing. DC 13 CON or cursed with weretiger lycanthropy.
Claw (tiger/hybrid): +5 to hit, 7 (1d8+3) slashing.
Longbow (humanoid/hybrid): +4 to hit, range 150/600, 6 (1d8+2) piercing.
Immunity: B/P/S from nonmagical, non-silvered attacks. 120 HP. Keen Hearing and Smell. Darkvision 60 ft.
Weretiger.tactical_identity: Ambushes with Stealth +4, pounces to knock prone (DC 14), bonus-action bites the downed target, then fights with claws or shifts to humanoid for longbow range. 120 HP with nonmagical immunity makes it incredibly durable. The weretiger is a solitary hunter, not a pack fighter — it picks off targets one at a time. Neutral alignment means it might be a reclusive guardian, a gladiator champion, or a jungle predator depending on context.
encounter_hooks: Pounce + nonmagical immunity combines the tiger's best offensive trait with lycanthropic invulnerability — a devastating solo hunter
Longbow capability at 150 ft. means the weretiger can fight at range in humanoid form, then shift to tiger for melee — tactically flexible
A neutral weretiger is as likely to test the party as to fight them — it respects strength and might become an ally if impressed
Weretiger.description: In hybrid form, a lithe, striped humanoid radiating predatory grace. A solitary hunter — patient, precise, and utterly silent until the pounce.
Weretiger.retreat: Vanishes into wilderness. An apex predator that cannot be tracked unwillingly.
Weretiger.social: Solitary and proud. Respects competence and independence. May offer to hunt alongside the party if they impress it.
aftermath_hooks: the weretiger was maintaining a balance in the region — with it gone, prey animals overpopulate or a rival predator moves in
the weretiger protected a specific place or person — that protection is now gone
a set of finely crafted weapons or equipment in its den suggest a sophisticated past life

Werewolf.signature: The iconic lycanthrope — nonmagical weapon immunity, a spreading bite curse, and pack hunting tactics that turn a village into a nightmare of suspicion and silver shortages.
abilities: Multiattack (humanoid/hybrid): Bite + Claws or Spear.
Bite (wolf/hybrid): +4 to hit, 6 (1d8+2) piercing. DC 12 CON or cursed with werewolf lycanthropy.
Claws (hybrid): +4 to hit, 7 (2d4+2) slashing.
Spear (humanoid): +4 to hit, 5 (1d6+2) or 6 (1d8+2) two-handed.
Immunity: B/P/S from nonmagical, non-silvered attacks. 58 HP. Keen Hearing and Smell.
Werewolf.tactical_identity: Hunts in packs in hybrid form, using Keen senses to track prey and nonmagical immunity to shrug off arrows and swords. Bite + claws for 13 per round is modest, but immunity to normal weapons means most NPCs deal zero damage. In wolf form, it infiltrates among normal wolves. In humanoid form, it hides among villagers. The horror is the mystery — who in the village transforms at night? The werewolf is CR 3, but the encounter is about investigation, not just combat.
encounter_hooks: Nonmagical weapon immunity means the party needs silver — finding or forging silver weapons IS part of the encounter design
The lycanthropy curse (DC 12 CON) on every bite turns combat into a horror scenario where getting hit has permanent consequences
A werewolf hidden among villagers is a mystery encounter — the investigation to find who transforms at the full moon is the real adventure
Werewolf.description: In hybrid form, a snarling, fur-covered humanoid with a wolf's head and claws dripping with menace. The iconic lycanthrope — hunts in packs, spreads the curse through its bite.
Werewolf.retreat: Reverts to humanoid form and blends into a crowd or settlement. Returns at the next full moon.
Werewolf.social: In humanoid form, may not know it's a werewolf. If aware, may beg for help or embrace the curse. In hybrid form, acts on rage and hunger.
aftermath_hooks: a bitten party member or NPC must deal with the curse before the next full moon
the werewolf's humanoid identity is someone the party or a settlement knows — the revelation has social consequences
the werewolf was part of a pack — the alpha is still out there, and pack members are embedded in the local population

Winter Wolf.signature: An intelligent wolf with Pack Tactics, a cold breath weapon, and the ability to speak Common and Giant — it's a tactical pack leader, not just a big wolf.
abilities: Bite: +6 to hit, 11 (2d6+4) piercing. DC 14 STR save or prone.
Cold Breath (Recharge 5-6): 15 ft. cone, DC 12 DEX, 18 (4d8) cold, half on save.
Pack Tactics: Advantage on attacks if ally within 5 ft.
Snow Camouflage: Advantage on Stealth in snow.
Cold immunity. Speaks Common and Giant. Speed 50 ft. Keen Hearing and Smell.
Winter Wolf.tactical_identity: Opens with Cold Breath on clustered targets for 18 damage, then charges in with Pack Tactics for advantage on a prone-knockdown bite. Speaks Common and Giant, meaning it coordinates with frost giant allies and can even negotiate or taunt the party. Snow Camouflage lets it ambush from snowdrifts. In a pack with regular wolves, the winter wolf commands while the wolves provide Pack Tactics flanking. It's the alpha predator of the arctic — smart, cold-immune, and tactical.
encounter_hooks: Speaking Common means the winter wolf can parley, threaten, or offer information — it's not a mindless beast, it's an intelligent adversary
Cold Breath + Pack Tactics with regular wolves creates a layered encounter — the winter wolf breathes, the wolves swarm the prone victims
Frost giant scouts ride winter wolves — a winter wolf patrol means frost giants are nearby, and the wolf reports back in Giant
Winter Wolf.description: A wolf the size of a dire wolf with white fur and frost forming on its breath. Intelligent enough to speak Common and Giant, and allied with frost giants — this is not a beast, it's a person.
Winter Wolf.retreat: Flees across frozen terrain. Howls to summon allies.
Winter Wolf.social: Speaks Giant and Common. Intelligent and cruel. Serves frost giants willingly. Can be negotiated with but considers most creatures prey.
aftermath_hooks: the winter wolf served a frost giant — its death will be reported when it fails to return
the cold breath has frozen something in place — a door, a mechanism, a pool of water
the wolf pack included regular wolves it commanded — the pack scatters without leadership

Worg.signature: The goblinoid war mount — speaks Goblin, has a prone-knockdown bite, and runs at 50 ft. making it the terrifying cavalry beast of goblin warbands.
abilities: Bite: +5 to hit, 10 (2d6+3) piercing. DC 13 STR save or prone.
Keen Hearing and Smell. Darkvision 60 ft. Speed 50 ft.
Speaks Goblin and Worg.
Worg.tactical_identity: A goblin rides a worg at 50 ft., the worg bites for 10 damage and knocks the target prone (DC 13), then the goblin attacks the prone victim from mounted advantage. Speaking Goblin means worgs coordinate with their riders — they're not animals following commands, they're intelligent partners planning ambushes. In packs, they run down fleeing targets at 50 ft. speed. Without a rider, a worg is still a dangerous predator that's smarter than a wolf.
encounter_hooks: Goblin-worg cavalry charges at 50 ft. with prone knockdown completely changes the dynamic of a goblin encounter — these aren't pushovers anymore
Worgs speak Goblin and are intelligent enough to plan — they're equal partners with their riders, not beasts of burden
A pack of worgs without riders might be scouting for a goblin warband — their presence signals a larger force nearby
Worg.description: A wolf the size of a horse with cruel intelligence in its eyes. Speaks Goblin and Worg — these are not animals, they are partners who choose to work with goblins and hobgoblins.
Worg.retreat: Flees when packmates are killed. Howls to alert nearby allies while retreating.
Worg.social: Speaks Goblin and Worg. Can be intimidated into backing down or bribed with fresh meat. Will betray goblinoid allies if offered better terms.
aftermath_hooks: tracks and scent trails leading to a goblinoid camp or lair nearby
one worg's collar bears markings identifying its goblin master or tribe
the worgs were guarding a path — what they were keeping the party from reaching matters more than the worgs themselves

Wyvern.signature: A flying dragon-kin with an 80 ft. fly speed and a stinger that deals 24 poison damage on a failed save — the poor man's dragon that dive-bombs with bite-and-sting for up to 46 damage in one turn.
abilities: Multiattack: Bite + Stinger. While flying, can replace one with Claws.
Bite: +7 to hit, reach 10 ft., 11 (2d6+4) piercing.
Stinger: +7 to hit, reach 10 ft., 11 (2d6+4) piercing + DC 15 CON or 24 (7d6) poison, half on save.
Claws (flying only): +7 to hit, 13 (2d8+4) slashing.
Fly 80 ft. 110 HP. AC 13.
Wyvern.tactical_identity: Dives from 80 ft. altitude, bites for 11 and stings for up to 35 (11 piercing + 24 poison). Total potential damage of 46 in one round makes it hit far above its CR 6 rating. DC 15 poison save is hard for most characters at this tier. It flies back up after attacking — without ranged options, the party can't retaliate. Low AC (13) means it's easy to hit when you CAN reach it, and 110 HP goes fast under focused fire. The wyvern is a glass cannon with altitude advantage.
encounter_hooks: DC 15 for 24 poison on the stinger makes every dive-bomb potentially devastating — one failed save can drop a mid-level character
80 ft. fly speed with no Flyby means it provokes opportunity attacks — but it's usually 40 ft. up before the next turn, out of melee reach
Wyverns as mounts for elite warriors (orcs, hobgoblins, knights) create aerial cavalry encounters that demand anti-air tactics
Wyvern.description: A dragon-like creature with two legs, bat wings, and a scorpion-like tail dripping venom. Smaller and stupider than a true dragon — a dive-bombing predator, not a schemer.
Wyvern.retreat: Flies away when wounded. Returns to its nest.
aftermath_hooks: the wyvern's nest contains eggs that are valuable to certain buyers — and dangerous to transport
the wyvern's tail stinger poison lingers — a stung character should be treated within hours
the wyvern was being used as a mount — the saddle and riding gear identify its rider

Yeti.signature: Chilling Gaze paralyzes on a failed DC 13 CON save — a free action included in its multiattack that can lock down one character every round while the yeti claws them to death.
abilities: Multiattack: Chilling Gaze + two Claw attacks.
Claw: +6 to hit, 7 (1d6+4) slashing + 3 (1d6) cold. 10 total per hit.

Chilling Gaze: 30 ft., one target that can see the yeti. DC 13 CON or 10 (3d6) cold damage and paralyzed 1 min (repeat save end of each turn). Immune to cold = immune to gaze. Success = immune to all yeti Chilling Gaze 1 hour.
Fear of Fire: Taking fire damage gives disadvantage on attacks and ability checks until end of next turn.
Cold immunity. Snow Camouflage. Climb 40 ft. Keen Smell.
Yeti.tactical_identity: Every turn: Chilling Gaze to paralyze one target (free with multiattack), then two claws at the paralyzed victim for automatic crits (20 damage as crits). Paralysis means melee attacks within 5 ft. are automatic critical hits — two claw crits deal 40 damage per turn to a paralyzed target, which kills most characters in 2 rounds. Snow Camouflage for ambush in blizzard conditions. Fear of Fire is the critical weakness — any fire damage gives it disadvantage on everything, making it dramatically less effective. Cold immune creatures are immune to the gaze entirely.
encounter_hooks: Chilling Gaze + claw crits on paralyzed targets is a death sentence: 40 damage per round from auto-crits drops characters in 2 turns — breaking the paralysis is priority one
Fear of Fire is a hard counter that reduces the yeti from terrifying to pathetic — a single torch or Fire Bolt changes the entire encounter
Snow Camouflage in a blizzard means the yeti ambushes from whiteout conditions — the party is blind while the yeti sees perfectly through Keen Smell
Yeti.description: A towering, white-furred ape-like creature whose gaze can paralyze with supernatural cold. An arctic ambush predator that fears fire — the campfire is both bait and ward.
Yeti.retreat: Flees into blizzard conditions where it has advantage. Tracks the party through snow.
aftermath_hooks: the yeti's Chilling Gaze paralyzed a victim who froze to death nearby — the frozen figure holds something
the yeti's cave contains frozen provisions — meat, but also supplies from a missing expedition
the yeti was avoiding something higher up the mountain — its territory was the lower, safer zone
